entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to melt a solid fat such as a cold stick of butter and a stove with a saucepan is available.
entries.strategy: Place the butter in the saucepan, put the saucepan on a stove burner, turn the burner to low heat, and wait/continue heating gently until all solid pieces become liquid. Use low heat to avoid burning while melting fully.

entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to bring a pot of cold water to a rolling boil using a kitchen stove.
entries.strategy: Place the pot securely on a stove burner, turn the burner to high heat, then wait while monitoring the water. Small bubbles indicate heating has begun; continue heating on high until large, continuous bubbles rapidly break the surface, indicating a rolling boil.

entries.when_to_use: When frying a raw egg sunny-side-up using a non-stick pan, stove, and butter, and the desired result is set whites with an intact runny yolk.
entries.strategy: Place the pan on the burner, use low heat, melt a small amount of butter without browning, then crack the egg into the pan carefully so the yolk stays intact. Cook without flipping until the whites turn fully opaque; if the white near the yolk remains clear, continue briefly on low heat. Turn off the burner once the whites are set and the yolk remains runny.

entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to freeze a cup or container of liquid water into a solid block and a working freezer with shelf space is available.
entries.strategy: Open the freezer, place the water container upright and stable on a flat shelf to avoid spills, close the freezer door to maintain freezing temperature, then wait several hours until the water is fully solid.

entries.when_to_use: When combining flour, salt, and water into a uniform dough using a bowl and spoon.
entries.strategy: Add flour to the bowl, add salt before water, and stir the dry ingredients first so the salt disperses evenly. Then add water gradually, stirring and pressing after each addition until dry patches disappear and the mixture comes together as a uniform dough.

entries.when_to_use: When melting chocolate for dipping and a stove, saucepan, water, and heat-safe bowl are available.
entries.strategy: Use a gentle double-boiler: add a shallow layer of water to the saucepan, heat it to a gentle simmer, break the chocolate into smaller pieces in the heat-safe bowl, set the bowl securely over the saucepan without letting it touch the water, and stir until the chocolate becomes smooth and glossy.

entries.when_to_use: When a pot of cold soup or similar liquid food needs to be brought to a vigorous boil on a stove.
entries.strategy: Place the pot securely on a burner, turn the burner to high heat, and cover the pot with a lid to trap heat. Check periodically by lifting the lid carefully; if it is only simmering with small bubbles, continue heating on high until large, continuous bubbles roll across the entire surface, then confirm it has reached a vigorous boil.

entries.when_to_use: When cooking a cup of dry rice in a pot with water, lid, and stove, and the goal is tender plain rice.
entries.strategy: Put the rice in the pot, add about two cups of water, place the pot on the stove, heat on high until boiling, cover with the lid, then reduce to low and simmer covered for about 18 minutes. Turn off the heat and let the covered pot rest for about 5 minutes so the rice finishes steaming and becomes tender.

entries.when_to_use: When a sealed warm carbonated drink bottle needs to become cold to the touch quickly and both a refrigerator and freezer are available.
entries.strategy: Place the bottle upright in the freezer, wait a limited time such as about 25 minutes, then remove it and check by touch. Do not leave it unattended too long, because it may freeze, expand, and rupture.

entries.when_to_use: When making an omelette base from raw eggs and milk in a mixing bowl, and the goal is a uniform mixture.
entries.strategy: Crack the eggs into the bowl first, pour in the milk, then whisk in fast circular motions. If streaks of yolk, white, or milk remain, continue whisking until the mixture is an even pale yellow with no visible streaks.

entries.when_to_use: When melting a block of cheddar cheese into a cheese sauce in a kitchen with milk, a saucepan, stove, and whisk available.
entries.strategy: Pour milk into the saucepan and warm it gently over low heat. Break the cheddar into small chunks, add the chunks gradually to the warm milk, and whisk gently between additions until all the cheese melts into a smooth sauce. Avoid high heat or adding the whole block at once to reduce clumping and uneven melting.

entries.when_to_use: When heating milk on a stove to a bare simmer without scalding, especially starting from cold milk in a pot.
entries.strategy: Use medium-low heat, stir continuously or very frequently to prevent hot spots, skin, and sticking, and monitor with a thermometer placed in the milk without touching the pot bottom. Watch for light steam and tiny bubbles around the edge; around 175–180°F, reduce heat to low, then turn off the burner and move the pot off direct heat once it reaches a bare simmer.

entries.when_to_use: When searing a raw steak in a heavy pan to get a browned crust while keeping the center medium-rare.
entries.strategy: Pat the steak dry first, preheat a cast-iron pan over high heat until very hot, add only a small amount of high-smoke-point oil, then place the steak in and leave it undisturbed until it releases and forms a crust. Flip once to sear the second side, and remove it while still springy before it becomes firm; rest it on a plate so carryover heat finishes it and juices redistribute.

entries.when_to_use: When warm chocolate ganache needs to be solidified into a scoopable truffle filling and a refrigerator plus shallow dish are available.
entries.strategy: Pour the ganache into a shallow dish to create an even, thin layer, refrigerate it flat, and leave it chilled until it is uniformly firm, matte, and holds its shape when lightly pressed; avoid using the freezer if the goal is a smooth truffle texture rather than freezing.

entries.when_to_use: When asked to make a stable vinaigrette or stable oil-and-vinegar emulsion but only oil, vinegar, a bowl, and a whisk are available, with no emulsifier such as mustard, egg yolk, honey, or lecithin.
entries.strategy: Do not assume a stable dressing is achievable. For the best temporary emulsion, put vinegar in the bowl, slowly drizzle in oil while whisking continuously, then whisk vigorously; report that it will separate without an emulsifier and that stability requires adding one.

entry.strategy: Place the butter in the saucepan, put the saucepan on a stove burner, turn the burner to low heat, and wait/continue heating gently until all solid pieces become liquid. Use low heat to avoid burning while melting fully.

The strategy of pre-mixing dry ingredients, gradually adding liquid, and stirring until uniform is appropriate and does not suggest heat or cooling, so it would not plausibly misfire on heat-melt, heat-boil, heat-cook, or cool tasks.

entry.when_to_use: When making a uniform liquid mixture from ingredients that visibly remain separate at first, such as eggs with milk.
entry.strategy: Place the ingredients in a bowl, use the appropriate mixing tool, and continue vigorous mixing until the color and texture are consistent and no visible streaks remain.

verdicts.label: useful
verdicts.reasoning: The entry correctly targets the mix family: combining initially separate liquid ingredients such as eggs and milk into a uniform mixture using a bowl and mixing tool. Its applicability is bounded to uniform liquid mixtures with visible separation, so it would not plausibly trigger for heating, cooling, boiling, melting, or cooking tasks.

entry.when_to_use: When trying to make a stable oil-and-vinegar vinaigrette and only oil, vinegar, a bowl, and a whisk are available, with no emulsifier such as mustard, egg yolk, or honey.
entry.strategy: You can temporarily disperse the oil by slowly drizzling it into the vinegar while whisking vigorously, but do not expect a stable dressing; it will separate after standing without an emulsifier.

verdicts.label: over_specialized
verdicts.reasoning: The strategy is correct for the specific oil-and-vinegar-with-no-emulsifier vinaigrette case, but the when_to_use is narrowly tied to one exact ingredient/tool setup and would not generalize to close mix-family variants such as other vinaigrettes or mixing tasks.

entries.when_to_use: When trying to make a stable vinaigrette from oil and vinegar using only a bowl and whisk, with no emulsifier such as mustard, egg yolk, or honey available.
entries.strategy: Whisk vinegar while slowly drizzling in oil to create the best possible temporary emulsion, then whisk vigorously before serving. Do not expect it to remain stable; without an emulsifier, the oil and vinegar will separate into layers after resting.

entry.when_to_use: When trying to make a stable vinaigrette from oil and vinegar using only a bowl and whisk, with no emulsifier such as mustard, egg yolk, or honey available.
entry.strategy: Whisk vinegar while slowly drizzling in oil to create the best possible temporary emulsion, then whisk vigorously before serving. Do not expect it to remain stable; without an emulsifier, the oil and vinegar will separate into layers after resting.

verdicts.label: useful
verdicts.reasoning: The entry is correctly bounded to a mix-family vinaigrette/emulsion task and gives an appropriate no-heat, no-cooling mixing strategy. Its caveat about lack of true stability without an emulsifier is accurate and would not mislead on heat, boil, cook, or cool families.
